did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on the ResNet architecture to classify skin lesions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign naevi) using reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) mosaics. The model achieved a classification accuracy of 87% on the test set, demonstrating the use of deep learning for automated dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on ResNet architecture to classify skin lesions using reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) mosaics. It details a specific dataset of 429 RCM mosaics, describes the methodology including data preprocessing, model training, and hyperparameter tuning, and reports new results such as an 87% classification accuracy on a test set. The authors explicitly state in the contribution section that they 'propose a system' and 'show results,' distinguishing it from a review of prior literature.

title: Convolutional Neural Network Approach to Classify Skin Lesions Using Reflectance Confocal Microscopy
brief_summary: This study proposes a Convolutional Neural Network based on the ResNet architecture to classify skin lesions using reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) mosaics. The dataset comprised 429 RCM mosaics divided into three classes: melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign naevi, with ground truth confirmed by histopathology. The model was evaluated using a 10-fold cross-validation technique on a test set comprising 10% of the data. The system achieved an overall classification accuracy of 87%, which was higher than the accuracy achieved by medical confocal users. The results demonstrate that the proposed deep learning approach can serve as a useful tool for aiding in early, non-invasive melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the dataset (167 cases), ground-truth confirmed by histopathological examination.
One of the three classes in the dataset (116 cases), ground-truth confirmed by histopathological examination.
One of the three classes in the dataset (146 cases), ground-truth confirmed by histopathological examination.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Naevi)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on ResNet architecture to classify skin lesions using reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) mosaics into three discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign naevi. The model was evaluated using classification accuracy (87% on test set), precision, recall, and F1-score. The task is explicitly described as 'classification' throughout the text.

datasets_used.name: Reflectance Confocal Microscopy (RCM) Mosaics Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of 429 RCM mosaics acquired using Vivascope 1500 (Mavig GmbH, Munich, Germany) from patients at the University of Modena and Reggio Emilia. Ground truth was confirmed by histopathological examination. Images were analyzed retrospectively and anonymized.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet-based CNN model. The dataset was split such that 10% of images from each class formed the test set, and the remaining 90% were divided into training and validation sets using 10-fold cross-validation. Data augmentation (affine transform, random cropping, contrast/brightness changes, flipping) was applied to the training data.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter optimization and model selection (selecting parameters with lowest validation error) via 10-fold cross-validation on the 90% non-test portion of the data.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model. The test set consisted of 10% of images randomly selected from each class, kept separate during learning and hyperparameter optimization. Results were averaged over 10 model evaluations.
use.other: The network was pre-trained on the ImageNet database before being fine-tuned on this RCM dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the non-test portion of the dataset (90%) into training and validation sets for hyperparameter optimization and model selection, and to evaluate the final model on the test set by averaging results over 10 model evaluations.
To calculate and report classification metrics (precision, recall, F1-score, support) for each class and overall performance.

To report the averaged classification results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrix values over the 10 model evaluations on the test set, providing an estimate of result variability.
Used as the cost function during model training to optimize network parameters.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported classification accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores with standard deviations derived from 10-fold cross-validation, but did not perform statistical hypothesis tests to compare the model's performance against medical experts or other baselines.

RCM Mosaics Dataset (Validation Set).description: Accumulated confusion matrix and classification summary for the validation set from all 10 folds.
RCM Mosaics Dataset (Validation Set).notes: The validation set consisted of 90% of the data divided via 10-fold cross-validation. The model with the lowest validation error was selected for final testing.

RCM Mosaics Dataset (Test Set).description: Averaged confusion matrix and classification summary for the test set (10% of data, 41 images) over 10 model evaluations.
RCM Mosaics Dataset (Test Set).notes: The test set accuracy (87%) was higher than the accuracy achieved by medical confocal users. The results were averaged over 10 model evaluations, each using a different fold for training.

preprocessing_techniques: Downsampling of high-resolution RCM mosaics (up to 12000x12000) to input sizes of 224x224, 448x448, or 896x896 pixels to make the architecture affordable and processing time acceptable.
Data augmentation on the training set including randomly applied affine transforms, random cropping, contrast and brightness linear changes, and vertical and horizontal flipping.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Randomly applied to the training set.
Randomly applied to the training set.
Linear changes randomly applied to the training set.
Vertical and horizontal flipping randomly applied to the training set.

conclusions: The proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on the ResNet architecture achieved a classification accuracy of 87% on the test set for distinguishing melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign naevi using reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) mosaics.
The AI system's classification accuracy (87%) was higher than the accuracy achieved by medical confocal users in previous literature.
The results demonstrate that the proposed deep learning approach can serve as a useful tool to aid in early, non-invasive melanoma detection.
The study provides an alternative to systems using dermatoscopy-based techniques for automatic classification of skin lesions.
The results show great potential for automatic classification systems of RCM images to improve the early detection of skin cancers, particularly melanoma.
The authors acknowledge that the results cannot be directly compared to medical experts because the patient population dataset is different.

The authors identify limitations in current architectures (ResNet, VGG, AlexNet, SqueezeNet, DenseNet) which suffered from similar issues and plan future work to propose a network architecture dedicated specifically to RCM images.
Future work includes proposing two separate networks: one to segment structures differentiating lesions and another for classification.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small dataset size (429 RCM mosaics), which the authors acknowledge strongly limits classification accuracy.
Results cannot be directly compared to medical experts because the patient population dataset used in this study is different from that of the referenced expert studies.
The test set is very small (only 41 images, 10% of the data), which may affect the statistical robustness of the reported 87% accuracy.
The network architecture was intentionally made shallower than typical ResNet models because extending it further did not improve performance.
The model was pre-trained on ImageNet (natural images) and fine-tuned; the authors note that this transfer learning improved results by more than 20% compared to training from scratch.
The study is retrospective and uses anonymized images from a single center (University of Modena and Reggio Emilia), limiting generalizability.
Future work is identified as necessary to develop a network architecture dedicated specifically to RCM images rather than adapting general architectures like ResNet.
The authors plan to split the task into separate segmentation and classification networks to handle artifacts better and potentially increase effective dataset size.
